sentences.text: That's right.
What was very hurtful to me is the fact to hear that Jews have killed Jesus Christ.
And this is why they are hated.
And this sort of fostered the further dislike of Jews.
Now, it was a very traumatic experience for me.
Number one, we're the only Jewish family, once my parents did get me back, my real parents.
So it truly was a very traumatic experience for me to hear that.
And I felt embarrassed.
I felt ashamed of having Jewish parents, even though I felt I wasn't a Jew.
I was christened.
I was baptized, yes.
So I felt that I should not be criticized like that.
But at the same time also, I could not understand.
The more I got to know my parents, the more I realized that they were very nice people, even though they were Jewish.
So I could not understand why should they be hated for something they didn't do.
What do you remember of that day when your mother returned?
That was probably the most traumatic experience for me to find out that your parents that you thought were your parents, are not your parents.
Your brother is not your brother.
It's a tremendous loss, and to find out on top of that that your real parents are Jewish.
And I was extremely upset about it, traumatized.
But I recall my mother used to come and tried to visit me as often as she could, before I was given back.
Because Stanczak family did not want to give me back, which was only understandable.
They loved me very much, and they didn't want to give me back.
However, I remember standing on a balcony that was after my mother started kissing me and hugging me, the first time I met her.
She just was coming to try-- and to try to visit me.
And the apartment was closed.
I would be standing on the balcony.
My other mother was gone.
And she closed me in the apartment, and I could get out to the balcony.
And I would watch my real mother downstairs.
And she would bring me food or something, and she would like to feed me and give me some toys or whatever.
And she couldn't, because she couldn't get to me.
I was on the balcony.
I-- I remember these moments.
And I sort of started liking her.
Because she was a nice lady.
She was bringing me food, and gifts, et cetera.
But I still didn't want to go to her.
And finally when the court case came about, and I remember holding on to the skirt of my other mother.
I didn't want to leave.
I was screaming, crying.
And [DRINKS] I had to be separated from her.
And I certainly didn't want to go.
My mother tells me.
I don't recall that at all.
But my mother keeps on telling me that during the case court, my other mother mentioned if I knew that I had a Jewish child, I would have killed her myself.
Now, I don't remember hearing this.
I don't know.
But this is what my mother tells me.
And I guess she was extremely jealous.
And I don't know whether it is true, whether it is not true.
But I don't recall any such thing.
And I only remember that I was very upset, getting back with my real mother to Krosniewice to that little town.
And I recall the first few visits of my other mother.
And I was running after her.
You know, when she was-- I remember, I recall one scene.
She came with-- I think it-- it was on a bus.
It was sort of an open truck.
And she came, and she walked on top of the truck.
And I started running after that truck, when she was leaving, and crying very much.
But that was just, you know, really in the beginning, when I was with my real parents.
And my mother didn't know what to do with me.
I mean, she was extremely nice to me.
[DRINKS] Understanding and she certainly allowed me to have all my pictures of the Jesus over my bed, and things like that.
So I just continued having, you know, the pictures praying to the only God I knew.
And as it turned out, both of my parents were not religious at all.
So it's not like I had something to learn from them, other than Roman Catholicism.
I didn't.
As a matter of fact my father hated religion with passion, my real father.
And my mother is completely, totally not religious at all.
So I never was shown anything else.
So I stayed basically with Roman Catholicism for the rest of the time I was in Poland.
And no matter how many times I would go to church and pray to God, whatever, I still was very discriminated against.
Kids were calling me a dirty Jew anyway.
And it wasn't a pleasant atmosphere to live in.
It's a very small town.
Everybody knows everybody.
And basically, you know, I recall some horrible things.
One time I recall when I was probably seven years old or so, one-- one of the people got really drunk, one of our neighbors.
Because our brownstone, we could not occupy just by ourselves.
We had to, under the Communists, you know, you just couldn't have more than two big rooms or something like that per family.
And we had to relinquish the rest of the building for other people to live in.
And as a result, we had some neighbors that were drunk.
And one of them got very drunk.
And he said-- he started screaming one night, if the Germans didn't kill you, I will finish you Jews.
And I remember my mother was terrified.
She put me, sort of hid me under a bed.
And the guy was just chopping at the door with a huge hammer, and-- and not a knife, it's sort of what you use in a forest, you know.
I forget the name of the instrument.
But he was just chopping the door down.
And I really was terrified.
Lucky my father was outside at the time, and he realized what was happening.
So he ran to the police, and brought in the police who stopped him.
But the door was almost gone by the time the police came.
So it was a horrifying moment.
It wasn't easy for me, really, to get to appreciate or like Jews.
After all, I was discriminated because my parents were Jewish.
And as a child, you-- you can't really think these things out.
One time I recall, which it's very embarrassing for me.
But I think it's something worthwhile telling you.
My mother was at a time with-- delivering my sister in Kutno.
And by that time, I was-- I was nine years old already, which means I was three and a half years with my parents.
And yet the feeling, you know, that these are Jews, you know, and I am not, was always there.
And of course, I was angry.
I was angry that here I'm coming.
There was no-- I mean, normally in any household, as you very well know, when you have another child coming along, the older one feels threatened.
And here, not only am I so confused with everything what's happening around me, but on top of that my mother is going to have another child.
So of course I was jealous.
I was upset.
And I was staying with my father alone.
And my mother was away, so it was Sunday.
I remember vividly.
People were starting to go to church.
And I wanted to go to church, as well.
And my father was trying to help to dress me, you know.
And there was some shoes that I would not want to wear.
Because this was a pair of shoes that actually he made them.
It's incredible.
This man could do anything.
Not only all sorts of, you know, mechanical things.
But he prepared-- he made shoes for me.
Because it was difficult during that time in post-war Poland to have a good pair of leather shoes.
So he made them.
And I refused to put them on, you know, put them on.
So he tried to force them on me, you know.
He was holding my leg.
And tried to put those shoes on.
And I started screaming, the Jew is killing me!
The Jew is killing me!
Now we lived right in front, as I showed you, right in front of the church.
And we were at the entrance, you know, this was a brownstone, which you could enter to your rooms on one little-- a few steps.
And all of a sudden two guys from outside are coming into the house, running in to help the poor Roman Catholic child.
Because the Jew is killing this poor child.
I-- my father was furious, was incredibly furious.
And he was very strong.
And I remember, like today, he picked these guys up when they came trying to help me.
And he just threw them off the staircase, both of them.
I was so terrified.
I thought he's gonna really kill me.
I hid.
I was so embarrassed, and I was so frightened.
So these are the sort of little things, you know, that-- that happened as a result of it.
I always-- I always prayed for not really being a Jew.
That something, some miraculous thing would, you know, I would just would show me that my parents are not Jewish either.
That we are fine, that we-- you know, we're just-- we're just normal like everybody else.
It was a very, very terrifying time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was so funny.
Because I remember, recall later on we got some package from someone who adopted me, children after the war were sort of adopted by some other people from United States or from South Africa.
And these people were just awfully nice.
And they sort of adopted me.
And they would send us packages in the beginning right after the war.
And some of it was matzah that I had never seen.
I had no idea what it was.
But my girlfriends and I loved it.
So we stuffed our-- our pockets with the matzah, and we'd go to church.
And we'd sit there, pray, and then eat matzah.
[LAUGHS] I had no idea what it was.
But we loved it.
So these are the sort of happenings, you know.
But I never-- I never thought anything about Jewish religion or anything.
I had no idea about it.
At what point did your attitude towards your parents begin to soften?
Oh--
I guess I started understanding more and more as I grew older that this whole thing really makes no sense.

And-- and it just-- it became apparent to me that the people who are so fanatical, Roman Catholics, they don't understand what's going on, just the same way as today we can say fanatical Jews, you know, are just outrageous, the same way I felt that the Roman Catholics that were so fanatical, were outrageous.
I started thinking about-- about it myself.
I got a little older, and-- and realizing that these were wonderful people.
And I hope more people would be as nice as they are.
Because my parents were extremely helpful in the town.
They were very well off.
My father was working very hard.
But he was extremely well off.
And he was always willing to help everybody.
My mother was always helping my friends' parents.
And I was very happy about it.
My mother used to give me wonderful sandwiches to go to take to school.
And I would just distribute them to my friends who couldn't have sandwiches like that.
And I just thought this was absolutely normal to do.
As a matter of fact, one of my friends that I haven't seen for all these years, when I came back to Poland two years ago, she came over to me.
And she reminded me about it.
She says, I-- you probably must really dislike me.
Because what happened was she went to my mother, and she complained to my mother that I don't eat, but I give everything away.
And she was one of the ones who I gave to, you know.
And yet, she went to my mother to complain about me.
So I was upset with her.
And she says, I hope you forgave me already for that.
So it was funny.
Because my mother then started making more sandwiches, so I could have some too.
[DRINKS]
Excuse me.
I-- are you talking about my parents?
My adoptive parents.
Like my real parents.
When I was in-- that was already in Krosniewice, when I lived with them.
And I saw that they were very nice people.
So I had-- I've changed my mind quite a bit.
I mean seeing how could these people actually kill Jews-- I mean Jesus.
It's just impossible.
They were too nice to kill anybody.
I really-- not-- not in Poland, not in Poland.
I never really considered myself Jewish in Poland.
And it was a traumatic experience for me, again, to leave Poland.
But basically, my father didn't want to leave Poland either.
Because the only way we could leave Poland was to go to Israel.
Even though we had all our documents for United States, we could not come directly to United States.
My father's brothers were here, the ones who survived, lived in New York, one in New York and one-- yes, both of them at the time live in New York.
So my father had the documents.
And the government would not allow us to leave.
They needed him very much.
But at the same time, my father didn't want to go.
You know, he didn't want to leave Poland, and especially when he found out that the only place he can go when Gomulka came to power, he allowed the Jews to leave Poland.
But you could only leave for Israel.
And at that time, when my parents finally decided to leave in 1956, they were stripped of their citizenship, and allowed to migrate to Israel.
I was actually the only one in the family who was not stripped of their citizenship.
Because I was still Roman Catholic.
So you can imagine the hypocrisy of-- of the Communist world.
I only found out about it when I tried to get a visa to go to Poland, when I was already in the United States.
And only then when I went to the Polish consulate, I found out that I am still a Polish citizen.
And I said, please do me a favor.
What should I do, you know, just to get rid of the citizenship?
Because I certainly was American citizen already.
And I didn't want to be a Polish citizen.
So I had to renounce my citizenship myself at that time.
[LAUGHS] So it was kind of funny.
And where did your family go from Poland?
From Poland, I mean perhaps, just before leaving, I mean it was amazing.
Because many, many of my-- of my friends, even the ones who called me dirty Jew, et cetera, were very jealous that I can leave Poland.
And however, I really did not want to go.
I was so terrified.
I was so terrified, especially to go to Israel.
I mean can you imagine to go to a country where you have only Jews?
I mean that-- that was a very terrifying thing for me.
Because being brought up the Jews are only parasites and Jews are bad people.
Even though my understanding was already a little different.
However, I still had that lingering effect on me.
So I was really very upset of going to Israel.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a very shocking experience to me to go to Israel, and see that no, not every Jew is rich, like my parents were.
Not every Jew is some-- you know, some kind of I don't know what.
But to me to see a Jewish policeman, Jewish street cleaner, a Jewish prostitute was something amazing.
And I was just so delighted to see that they are just normal people like everybody else.
There are good Jews.
There are bad Jews.
I was shocked.
So it was a very, very good experience for me, I think.
Slow process, but definitely something very positive.
I enjoyed being there very much, and not in the beginning.
Because it was a very tough thing.
My parents didn't know at first what to do when I arrived.
Because I had two years of high school.
I was 15 years old when I arrived to Israel.
And I did not know Hebrew, of course.

So the recommendation was that the best thing for me to-- for them to do is to send me to mossad, where I can go to an ulpan, learn Hebrew, live there, and this way I would be sort of totally immersed, instead of living with them, but be totally immersed in with Jews and with Hebrew.
And that would be helpful to me.
So this is exactly what they did.
However, they had no idea of, you know, which ulpan.
So they accepted whatever ulpan was available.
And the one ulpan that was available at the time, I can't even recall the name of it right now.
But this ulpan had kids my age, and perhaps year older or so from Africa.
So there were no European Jews with exception of me and another girl.
And we were in this ulpan.
Now there was such a big difference, cultural shock, between us and these other kids that it was unbelievable.
Now, we were extremely naive at the age of 15.
We would not go out with boys.
This was something, I mean, you were just-- I was still a little girl, really, never wore any makeup, no lipstick, no high-heeled shoes, no-- I mean this was just very naive little kids really.
And all of a sudden, you know, one night we're there, you know, the first like-- it was maybe the second night or so.
These girls and boys would get together in the middle of the night, and they would be making love and stuff.
And they would have fights.
And they fought over some girl that we had no idea, in our room.
Me and my other friend from Poland, we started, we were so terrified, so scared, we hid under our beds.
And the guys started slashing each other because of that girl.
We were totally-- I mean, dumbfounded.
It's-- it was something unbelievable.
And after two hours of this whole thing, you know, finally someone, some adult heard, and came around and separated the group, and started collecting the injured, and et cetera.
And it was a terrifying thing for me.
The following day, you know, I-- I just-- I was very, very quiet.
But I said, I'm not going to go to that room again.
And after-- I must have been there just perhaps one week, I called my mother.
And my mom-- as a matter of fact, my mother called me just to find out how I'm doing.
And I-- and I was just saying to her, well, I'm fine.
Because I was embarrassed to say anything to her.
And she felt that there was something wrong.
And the next day she came to pick me up.
And I was crying like a little kid, and my friend was all upset because nobody came to fetch her.
You know, and so this was my experience with the mossad.
But after that, I went to an ulpan in the city, in Tel Aviv.
And things were very different.
None, except praying still, you know.
Whenever I would go to bed, I always said my prayers, which I only knew, you know, and that was it.
Oh, yes.

I-- at least, again unfortunately none of-- none of the people I was surrounded by-- I-- it's very interesting that not until I think year ago, for the first time when I was visiting my family in Israel that I went to a synagogue there.
I was never in a synagogue in Israel before.
And to me, it was such a shocking thing.
Because I have gone a few times in United States to a synagogue.
And of course synagogue here is very different.
I mean, women sit together with men.
It's-- the rabbi is a female, you know.
Things like that which just are not in Israel, you know.
This is not something that you see in Israel.
But I've never seen it until last year, when I went for the wedding of my cousin's daughter.
I went to a regular synagogue there.
And that was just before the marriage.
And they had some ceremony for not the bride, but for the bridegroom.
And it was interesting for me to come.
And I was like, I knew that women were sitting separately.
But I mean here, everybody was sitting upstairs.
The women were upstairs.
The guys were downstairs.
It was just a totally different feeling.
And I-- I knew about it.
I heard about it.
But I've never seen it, never felt it.
And for the first time I said to myself, my God, why are they so fanatic?
You know, and I wish that they would be a little more like the ones that I've seen in United States.
Because it makes me-- it doesn't make me feel good sitting there totally isolated, like I'm some kind of, you know, second-class citizen.
So I didn't like that feeling at all.
And-- but I was not-- let's say, when I lived in Israel, I-- I was not really seeing that part of Israel.
Because my cousin, for example, was a-- he's a-- he was a general of the paratroopers.
And I remember going with him in some parts of Jerusalem where they actually threw stones at us.
Because he was dressed in military uniform.
And he was in an open jeep.
And I guess it must have been during the time that he was not supposed to be around there, or something, when he drove over.
And they did throw stones at us.
I never had anything, you know, overly revealing clothing wise.
But I had short sleeves.
They didn't like that either.
So it's not that-- you know that-- I-- I enjoy Israel very much, and I like Israel very much.
And I'm very proud of-- of Israelis, and et cetera.
But the religious part I stay away from.
